ATLAS/CODEX EJS

[FR] Liste des plantes et arbres biomathematiques
[EN] List of biomathematical plants and trees

[FR] Le Codex des nombres premiers (une forêt) n'est pas dévoilé ici, toutefois il est déjà possible d'énumérer un certain nombre de plantes et d'arbres de ce codex avec leurs branches au travers de nombres qui décrivent partiellement ces arbres ou ces plantes, mais pour des raisons de confidentialité et de droits d'auteur les formules mathématiques permettant par exemple de construire les arbres ne sont pas encore fournies.


[EN] The Codex of Prime Numbers (a forest) is not revealed here, however it is already possible to enumerate a number of plants and trees from this codex with their branches through numbers that partially describe these trees or plants, but for reasons of confidentiality and copyright the mathematical formulas allowing for example to construct the trees are not yet provided.


X Y [FR] Liste des branches (feuilles, espece)
[EN] List of branches (leaves, species)

1600 40 13(44, 1), 17(68, 1), 23(52, 1), 29(51, 1), 41(117, 1), 53(61, 1), 59(82, 1), 83(77, 1), 169(36, 5), 269(48, 5), 419(74, 5), 1019(108, 5)

Spectrum of harmonics of B under parity constraint

Some large div scans can be replaced by targeted div
ABDiv (species)
11[2]
42[1]
93[2, 6]
164[1]
255[2, 10]
366[1, 3]
497[2, 14]
648[1]
819[2, 6]
10010[1, 5]
12111[2, 22]
14412[1, 3]
16913[2, 26]
19614[1, 7]
22515[2, 6, 10]
def generer_sequence_DIV(n): # valable uniquement si a=b*b
    def partie_impaire(nombre): # take off all the factors 2 of the number.
        while nombre > 0 and nombre % 2 == 0:
            nombre //= 2
        return nombre
    def liste_diviseurs(nombre): # Trouve tous les diviseurs du nombre entier.
        return [d for d in range(1, nombre + 1) if nombre % d == 0]
    if IsPrime(n):
        if n==2: return [1]
        else: return [2, 2*n]
    else:
        p_imp = partie_impaire(n)
        divs = liste_diviseurs(p_imp)
        if n % 2 != 0: # Cas Impair non NP, on multiplie par 2
            #print(f"{n} {divs}")
            return [2 * d for d in divs if 2 * d <= n+1] # 
        else: # Cas pair 
            return [d for d in divs] #  if d <= n
This sequence creates a bridge between the multiplicative structure of a number (its divisors) and its parity, while imposing a neighborhood constraint (n+1). The mathematical interest lies in its transformation of an integer "n" into a set of "resonances":
  • Even/Odd duality: Even numbers act as "reducers" (only the odd core is retained), while odd numbers act as "multipliers" (the divisors are doubled). Prime isolation: By forcing prime numbers to return [2, 2n], you create breakpoints in the sequence.
  • A classic sequence of divisors is monotonic in terms of size growth; this sequence "jumps" and "bounces," resembling certain dynamical systems or signal processing.
  • Self-limitation: The condition 2 * d <= n + 1 for odd numbers prevents the sequence from diverging. It is a self-regulating system.

First div sequence: 2, 1, 2, 6, 1, 2, 10, 1, 3, 2, 14, 1, 2, 6, 1, 5, 2, 22, 1, 3, 2, 26, 1, 7, 2, 6, 10, 1, 2, 34, 1, 3, 9, 2, 38, 1, 5, 2, 6, 14, 1, 11, 2, 46, 1, 3, 2, 10, 1, 13, 2, 6, 18, 1, 7, 2, 58, 1, 3, 5, 15, 2, 62, 1, 2, 6, 22, 1, 17, 2, 10, 14, 1, 3, 9, 2, 74, 1, 19, 2, 6, 26, 1, 5, 2, 82, 1, 3, 7, 21, 2, 86, 1, 11, 2, 6, 10, 18, 30, 1, 23, 2, 94, 1, 3, 2, 14, 1, 5, 25, 2, 6, 34, 1, 13, 2, 106, 1, 3, 9, 27, 2, 10, 22, 1, 7, 2, 6, 38, 1, 29, 2, 118, 1, 3, 5, 15, 2, 122, 1, 31, 2, 6, 14, 18, 42, 1, 2, 10, 26, 1, 3, 11, 33, 2, 134, 1, 17, 2, 6, 46, 1, 5, 7, 35, 2, 142, 1, 3, 9, 2, 146, 1, 37, 2, 6, 10, 30, 50, 1, 19, 2, 14, 22, 1, 3, 13, 39, 2, 158, 1, 5, 2, 6, 18, 54, 1, 41, 2, 166, 1, 3, 7, 21, 2, 10, 34, 1, 43, 2, 6, 58, 1, 11, 2, 178, 1, 3, 5, 9, 15, 45, 2, 14, 26, 1, 23, 2, 6, 62, 1, 47, 2, 10, 38, 1, 3, 2, 194, 1, 7, 49, 2, 6, 18, 22, 66, 1, 5, 25
Second div sequence: 2, 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 3, 2, 1, 2, 6, 1, 5, 2, 1, 3, 2, 1, 7, 2, 6, 10, 1, 2, 1, 3, 9, 2, 1, 5, 2, 6, 14, 1, 11, 2, 1, 3, 2, 10, 1, 13, 2, 6, 18, 1, 7, 2, 1, 3, 5, 15, 2, 1, 2, 6, 22, 1, 17, 2, 10, 14, 1, 3, 9, 2, 1, 19, 2, 6, 26, 1, 5, 2, 1, 3, 7, 21, 2, 1, 11, 2, 6, 10, 18, 30, 1, 23, 2, 1, 3, 2, 14, 1, 5, 25, 2, 6, 34, 1, 13, 2, 1, 3, 9, 27, 2, 10, 22, 1, 7, 2, 6, 38, 1, 29, 2, 1, 3, 5, 15, 2, 1, 31, 2, 6, 14, 18, 42, 1, 2, 10, 26, 1, 3, 11, 33, 2, 1, 17, 2, 6, 46, 1, 5, 7, 35, 2, 1, 3, 9, 2, 1, 37, 2, 6, 10, 30, 50, 1, 19, 2, 14, 22, 1, 3, 13, 39, 2, 1, 5, 2, 6, 18, 54, 1, 41, 2, 1, 3, 7, 21, 2, 10, 34, 1, 43, 2, 6, 58, 1, 11, 2, 1, 3, 5, 9, 15, 45, 2, 14, 26, 1, 23, 2, 6, 62, 1, 47, 2, 10, 38, 1, 3, 2, 1, 7, 49, 2, 6, 18, 22, 66, 1, 5, 25


ERIC JACOB - EJS - Exploring infinite deterministic sequences of prime numbers
